data.title: Freegold Ventures Is Well-Positioned Amid Promising Drilling In Alaska And Bullish Gold
data.description:
A Buy Rating for Freegold Ventures Limited

Thanks to a strong positive correlation with the price of gold, the US shares of Freegold Ventures Limited (OTCQX:FGOVF), a Vancouver, Canada-based company exploring precious and base metals, have so far benefited noticeably from the rapid rise in gold prices. The bullish trend is supported by drilling results that reveal very promising prospects for increasing gold resources at the flagship Golden Summit project, just a 30-minute drive from Fairbanks. This project is gaining momentum in light of the record price, and this interest is driving Freegold Ventures Limited shares ever higher, with a growth rate that is enviable even among much more reputable and established companies in the global gold exploration and mining industry.

Freegold Ventures Limited’s Performance Compared to the Global Industry

Thanks to this price rally, Freegold Ventures Limited, listed on the US OTCQX market, gained 147.25%, outperforming the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX)’s plus-59.11% over the past 12 months. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF Index is the benchmark for the performance of publicly traded gold exploration and mining companies, globally operating.

At the bottom of the TradingView chart, there is a yellow area curve representing a positive correlation between the company and the gold price. This relationship means that when gold prices rise, FGOVF's share price is likely to be in the uptrend mode, and when gold prices fall, FGOVF's share price is probably moving downwards. This is the essence of the positive correlation. This information is useful to know because the forecasts by the analysts (as we illustrate later) continue to push the gold price higher, encouraged by a favorable environment for shares of Freegold to keep on prospering. As drilling at the Golden Summit project picks up pace and reveals expandable and upgradeable gold ore deposits, FGOVF shares are expected to extend the previous period of growth.

The Gold Price Outlook Plus Forecasts

Let's look at the most common gold price benchmark.

The Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is $3,376.71 per ounce, representing an increase of 26.73% year-to-date and 44.61% over the past 12 months, at the time of this writing. This increase is solely due to demand for safe-haven investments, as the catalyst of hopes of an interest rate cut is currently not in operation.

The upside in the price of gold is primarily driven by demand for safe-haven assets, as investors seize the opportunity for appreciation of the troy ounce during times when macroeconomic uncertainty and geopolitical tensions threaten the value of their portfolios.

Hopes for lower interest rates in the US also typically contribute to a positive sentiment towards gold, as lower interest rates dampen demand for US Treasury bonds, which in turn benefits gold, which, in contrast, does not yield interest. This positive catalyst is currently missing, as the US Federal Reserve left interest rates unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% in June 2025 for the fourth consecutive month. This is because US Fed policymakers prefer to play it safe, as they cannot assess the impact of tariffs on the US economic outlook. We suspect US Fed policymakers don't want to make the mistake of underestimating the tariffs, as Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell said:

"What we've learned is that ... tariffs are going to be substantially larger than forecasters generally thought,"

AsYahoo Finance reported in its article “Fed Chair Powell: 'Substantially larger' tariffs are raising inflation expectations” on Wednesday, June 18, 2025, at 9:51 PM GMT+2.

The market narrative now suggests that the Fed will cut the federal funds rate twice, each by 25 basis points, before the end of 2025. CME FedWatch, which tracks the probabilities of Fed interest rate changes based on 30-day fed funds futures prices, estimates the probability of a 25 basis point cut to a range of 400–425 at 57.3% at the time of writing, down from 59.4% a week ago. CME FedWatch forecasts a 42.1% probability of a further 25 basis point cut to the 375-400 range, slightly higher than the 40.2% forecast a week ago, at the time of this writing.

On Wall Street, it's not all interest rate cuts; it appears that analysts don't expect any interest rate cuts this year. This is weighing on the Fed's interest rate optimism, which, as mentioned, already appears less enthusiastic than before the Fed's unchanged interest rate decision on June 18. This market sentiment regarding interest rates, which revolves around the Fed's continued “wait-and-see” stance, probably means that the gold price is currently not really benefiting from expectations of lower interest rates.

The Gold Price Forecasts

Regarding the gold price forecasts as published in the Equity Insider News Commentary on May 27, 2025, 9:50 AM ET, on behalf of RUA GOLD Inc. (RUA:CA) via PRNewswire and reported by Seeking Alpha:

Safe-haven demand, rate cut expectations, and fresh volatility tied to US President Donald Trump's tariff warnings have sent bullion prices soaring, prompting Citi to raise its short-term gold target to $3,500. Bank of America followed by increasing its gold exposure in Q4, citing a bullish shift in underlying market conditions.

[…]

Veteran investor Rob McEwen and billionaire John Paulson both see $5,000 gold within reach, while JPMorgan recently projected a potential path to $6,000 if even a sliver of U.S.-held foreign assets are reallocated toward bullion.

About Freegold Ventures Limited's Gold Mineral Interest In Alaska

In its report “Freegold Provides Update on 2025 Drill Program”, released on May 29, 2025, at 5:00 AM ET and published by Seeking Alpha, the company describes itself as follows:

Freegold is a TSX-listed company focused on exploration in Alaska. It holds the Golden Summit Gold Project near Fairbanks and the Shorty Creek Copper-Gold Project near Livengood through leases.

Freegold Ventures Limited's portfolio of gold mineral resources is well-positioned under US President Donald Trump, as his Administration 2.0 promotes the exploitation of domestic mineral resources.

As Seeking Alpha reported on March 24, 2025, at 12:52 PM ET, citing another company, on March 20, 2025,

President Trump signed an executive order calling for increased U.S. mineral production,

In our view, this order also improves the prospects for the Alaska projects of Freegold Ventures Limited, particularly the Golden Summit project, which appears to be more advanced than Shorty Creek.

In the just mentioned report, Seeking Alpha added:

The order focuses on securing a stable supply of essential minerals such as copper and gold for defense, technology, and infrastructure, while directing federal agencies to expedite domestic mineral project approvals.

President Trump's Executive Order is giving Freegold Ventures Limited shares a positive boost because the state of Alaska, where Golden Summit and Shorty Creek are located, is both a mining-friendly area and has abundant natural resources. These resources could become a huge reservoir of wealth for the U.S. economy through the mining activities of operators like Freegold Ventures.

Sprott’s Mining Risk Map shows that Alaska, like North America, has a very mining and exploration-friendly jurisdiction and environment, resulting in very little, if any, investment risk. Sprott is a global asset manager with decades of experience in physical investments in metals and minerals, as well as close relationships and expertise in the world of the mining industry.

The reduced or even non-existent investment risk is primarily reflected in the lower financing costs for developing, operating and expanding metal production resources. This future scenario of falling borrowing costs could be unattractive for lenders looking to raise capital at the most favorable terms. The US central bank may take a more accommodative stance in the future than it does now, suggesting a shift in the signal that could encourage Freegold to borrow and accelerate its gold projects.

The current stance of US policymakers continues to lead to excessively high borrowing costs and suggests a continued heightened risk of default for businesses and households struggling to make ends meet near the end of the month. Instead, with interest rates expected to cool from September onwards, Freegold's prospects for success in Alaska may gradually improve, opening the door to substantial external financing – capital requests that lenders are unlikely to turn down for three reasons:

A) because of President Trump’s executive orders to develop the United States’ domestic mineral resources and reduce the current over-reliance on foreign resources.

B) because conditions must be restored to stimulate economic growth in the United States, which, according to the US GDP, contracted for the first time in three years in the first quarter of 2025. This is, according to Trading Economics, the status of the GDP in the US at the time of this writing, based on sources from the US Bureau of Economic Analysis.

C) because, with gold prices perching at record-high levels, borrowing liquidity to finance these projects is inherently a profitable investment for lenders, even if the market imposes significantly lower interest rates. A higher gold price in the future is consistent with a vision that foresees an increasing demand for gold bullion as a safe asset, coinciding with a likely turbulent, threatening and tense global environment.

The State of Alaska Is Rich In Natural Resources

Alaska is undoubtedly rich in natural resources, and its impressive economic power can be extremely beneficial to the American economy and Alaskan society, as evidenced by some of the statistics we present here. Freegold Ventures is well positioned in this context, as it could be one of the players involved in the case to dig up the stored wealth.

Alaska is the second most gold-producing state in the U.S., says the Bureau of Land Management of Alaska.

The U.S. Geological Survey Mineral Commodity Summaries 2024 report shows that gold, primarily but also copper and other metals, together, as Alaska's production of non-hydrocarbon minerals is the sixth largest in the United States in U.S. dollar terms.

Golden Summit Gold Project Near Fairbanks

The Golden Summit Project is located 28 km northeast of Fairbanks, Alaska. Mineral resources are estimated to be primarily derived from the Dolphin/Cleary resource, as the Dolphin area is largely unexplored.

The Dolphin-Cleary gold deposit contains 13.106 million ounces of gold in indicated mineral resources at an average grade of around 1.04 grams per tonne of ore and 11.339 million ounces of gold in inferred mineral resources at an average grade of around 1.07 grams per tonne of ore, based on a September 2024 estimate, with a further update to be completed before June 30, 2025.

Golden Summit was scheduled to resume drilling after its winter break in mid-May, marking the start of a comprehensive 2025 drilling program. Based on the drilling results, particularly from the WOW Zone, a resource expansion target extending 1,500 meters west of the currently defined Dolphin Cleary deposit, the company expects both growth and improvement in resource grade. In one of its drilling reports dated April 23, 2025, Freegold Ventures Limited informed the market about the 2025 drilling campaign and wrote:

[…] This program will commence with two drill rigs and increase to four rigs by early summer, with a total of approximately 30,000 meters planned. All four rigs are currently on site. The program aims to be comprehensive, focusing on converting inferred mineral resources into the indicated category and drilling additional metallurgical holes in preparation for a planned Pre-Feasibility Study (PFS).

A preliminary feasibility study will be of great importance and Freegold Ventures shares could receive a boost upon its release, as it will provide not only technical but also some economic details on the development of a mine at Golden Summit.

The presence of Kinross Gold Corporation's (KGC) (K:CA) Fort Knox Mine just 6 km to the south certainly bodes well for the future construction of a prolific gold mine in the Golden Summit project area, centered on the Dolphin/Cleary resources, as the Fort Knox Mine has produced over 9 million ounces to date. The Dolphin/Cleary gold resource basin has a mineralization system similar in age to Kinross Gold's Fort Knox.

In its first quarter 2025 results, Kinross Gold reports that gold equivalent production at Fort Knox (approximately 22% of the total 512,088 gold equivalent ounces produced by the company in the first quarter of 2025) is increasing (+110% year-on-year and +7% quarter-on-quarter), supported by the commencement of Manh Choh production in the second half of 2024. This confirms the expandability of the gold resources in this district. In addition, costs continue to decline: Fort Knox's production costs per ounce sold reached $1,176 in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $1,466 in the first quarter of 2024. Total production costs per ounce sold in the US segment were $1,240 in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $1,312 in the first quarter of 2024. The company's total attributable production costs were $1,038 per ounce in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $982 per ounce in the first quarter of 2024.

Inferred or indicated mineral resources at Dolphin/Cleary deposits are not economically provable. Therefore, they must first pass the assessment to be included in the higher category of measured mineral reserves and ultimately in the highest category of proven or at least probable mineral reserves. Only then will it become clear how much the Dolphin/Cleary gold mine could produce and at what rates of return and cost.

About 125 kilometres by road northwest of Fairbanks lies Freegold Ventures' other project, called "Shorty Creek", a copper-gold project, which, however, is still at a very early stage compared to Golden Summit.

Raising Capital To Finance Mineral Resource Updates: An Indication of the Intrinsic Value of Freegold Ventures Limited Shares

Freegold finances exploration and related activities with capital from the issuance of common shares or the potential conversion of warrants into common shares. On April 03, the company closed the agreement with Paradigm Capital for a proposed private placement, which is expected to raise up to ≈ C$42 million (≈US$30.6 million as of this writing). The C$42 million will be raised through an offering of 49,383,300 units, each valued at C$0.85 price. This offering provides a rough estimate of the intrinsic value of one common share of Freegold Ventures, as each unit entitles the holder to one common share and one-half warrant to acquire one common share. Investors should note that the parties, including the financier, Paradigm Capital, have agreed to an exercise price of C$1.30 per warrant share, exercisable for a period of 24 months from the closing date. Since math is not an opinion, the growth potential is substantial and is backed by the continued whipsaw soar in the gold price and the development of gold mineral resources through drilling. The Golden Summit gold project is valuable, and the goal of the drillers is to establish an estimate of the ounces that can be profitably mined in the future.

Cash Burn and Cash Runaway

The company must cover general and administrative expenses of ≈ US$350,000 and exploration and evaluation costs of ≈ US$765,000 (as of Q1 2025). The latter cost category is capitalized and has so far resulted in a balance sheet total assets of US$107.3 million as of the first quarter of 2025. Because Freegold Ventures Limited is a gold explorer and not yet a gold producer, the company does not sell any ounces of gold and therefore does not generate any revenue. This results in a loss of ≈ $333,000 for the first quarter of 2025, which is ≈ equivalent to non-capitalizable general and administrative expenses.

In its Q1 2025 quarterly report, Freegold Ventures states:

As of March 31, 2025, based on current projections, the Company’s working capital of $6,575,977 is sufficient to meet its planned business objectives for the next 12 months.

The working capital of US$6,575,977 represents current assets (including cash and cash equivalents of US$6,618,383) less short-term liabilities of US$257,023 (primarily accounts payable).

General and administrative expenses of ≈ US$350,000 plus exploration and evaluation costs of ≈ US$765,000 (as of Q1 2025) amount to US$1.115 million. Multiplied by four quarters, this equals $4.460 million. Thus, working capital is effectively sufficient to cover the next twelve months of operations and an expected two additional quarters, assuming costs remain roughly at the same level as in the first quarter of 2025.

In its Q1 2025 quarterly report, Freegold Ventures adds:

The Company has no operating revenues and therefore must utilize its funds from financing transactions to maintain its capacity to meet ongoing operating activities.

As previously mentioned, an additional C$42 million will then come from the brokered private placement financing that closed after the first quarter of 2025. This amount of proceeds, without taking into account the additional capital raised through the exercise of the warrants, should provide spending autonomy for the next few years based on current projections of the costs Freegold Ventures will need to incur to achieve its planned business objectives.

In a few years, borrowing on the capital markets could become cheaper if the U.S. Federal Reserve further cuts interest rates after a possible resumption of this policy in the second half of 2025. If so, the company will likely also receive financing from willing lenders, provided that the Golden Summit project in Alaska has grown into an economically viable category of mineral resources through successful drilling by then.

The Stock Price: Look For The Dips

At the time of writing, Freegold Ventures Limited shares are trading on the US OTCQX under the symbol “FGOVF” at a price of US$ 0.80 per share, representing a market capitalization of US$ 428.57 million.

The stock price is volatile and could reach lower levels than the current ones, which are well above the middle point of $0.635/share of the 52-week range of $0.28/share to $0.99/share and above the MA Ribbon.

In fact, a 14-day RSI of 51.18, indicating neutrality relative to overbought/oversold status, suggests that there is enough room to the downside for shares to reach remarkably lower levels, which, if exploited, would be a way to offset potential headwinds arising from the fact that it is a low-liquidity stock.

The average volume of this stock over the last three months is 171,938 shares changing hands, out of a total of 528.97 million shares outstanding. Trading efficiency may be compromised if a sudden change in circumstances requires a rapid adjustment of the position, but the desired size is not achieved, or if achieved results are unsatisfactory because too many shares are in the position compared to the exchange's daily capacity to process the transactions.

The stock price experienced two sharp declines in recent months, with the RSI falling to oversold levels:

The first occurred in December 2024, as Seeking Alpha reported on December 6, 2024, and wrote:

Spot gold, up +0.11% to $2,636.74 an ounce on Friday, was set for a second-weekly loss. Bullion posted a monthly loss of 3.4% in November following Donald Trump's election win which lifted the U.S. dollar, curbing investor appetite in the metal.

The second drop occurred in early April 2025 in the wake of a surrender price for gold, according to Seeking Alpha, which wrote:

Gold prices pared some gains on Thursday as investors took profit amid negative sentiment in the broader market, while India's $32 billion gems and jewellery industry faces a sharp decline due to U.S. tariffs.

[…]

In the physical market, meanwhile, the U.S. government's decision to impose a 26% reciprocal tariff on Indian imports is expected to significantly impact India's gems and jewellery sector.

Gems and jewellery made up 12.8% of India's exports to the U.S. and amounted to $9.9 billion in FY24, and experts believe the retaliatory tariffs will lead to a sharp decline in India's diamond and jewellery exports.

The first negative catalyst that could lead to a decline in the share price and fuel the Buy case for Freegold Ventures is unlikely to materialize, as a stronger US dollar will dampen the process of reducing the US trade deficit. This had to fall from a record deficit of US$138.3 billion in March 2025, and a weaker US dollar helped it shrink to US$61.6 billion in April 2025, the lowest level since September 2023. We do not believe that the forces in the currency markets will abate, especially now that the US GDP contracted in the first quarter of 2025. Among other things, a stronger dollar would undermine efforts to limit capital outflows from the US, for example, through protectionist measures such as raising tariffs on US imports.

The second negative catalyst could occur, sending stock prices plummeting to oversold levels. Fears of larger and broader-than-expected tariffs imposed could prompt the Fed to wait and see the impact on inflation. If US policymakers are so fearful of a resurgence of rapid price increases, they could be setting the market up for a delayed restart with rate cuts in 2025, or possibly no rate cuts at all in 2025. And since gold, with a zero yield, will be unwilling to return to the ring to compete with the higher yields, driven by a positive correlation with the yellow metal, shares of Freegold Ventures will feel the effects.

The Risk Section

First, as mentioned above, the stock's low liquidity poses a risk.

Second, Freegold Ventures shares are rising rapidly and performing well in the GDX index due to their positive correlation with record-high sentiment towards the yellow metal. But if geopolitical conflicts and various tensions around the world subside, that is, if both or one of the major conflicts, Ukraine or the Middle East, cease, gold will significantly lose its current attractiveness as a safe haven investment. Freegold Ventures shares could then face strong headwinds that slow or block their growth.

Third, as of this writing, Trading Economics wrote about gold:

Gold fell to around $3,350 per ounce on Friday, hitting a one-week low and heading for its first weekly decline in three, as investors sold bullion to cover losses in other markets amid escalating Middle East tensions.

If investors repeat this behavior in light of a possible US military intervention against Iran to ease tensions in the Middle East, the price of gold could fall sharply again. Market participants could then view Freegold Ventures shares as overvalued and disproportionate to the lower gold price, potentially triggering negative sentiment toward the share price.

Fourth, sustained inflation, as the enactment of tariffs can drive up prices, could limit interest rate cuts. Higher-for-longer interest rates will weigh on non-yielding gold bars, and due to the positive correlation, shares of Freegold Ventures Limited will also come under pressure.

Conclusion

Driven by rising gold prices and the resulting enthusiasm for gold deposits, shares of Vancouver, Canada-based Freegold Ventures Limited skyrocketed as the company focused on the exploration and development of its Golden Summit project in mineral-rich Alaska, boosted by President Trump’s executive order to increase the use of U.S. mineral resources. Freegold Ventures’ shares outperformed many other stocks, even the best-known and largest. These catalysts are expected to continue to have a positive impact in the coming months, as is Freegold Ventures’ progress with drilling activities to expand its gold resources at the Golden Summit project, just a 30-minute drive from Fairbanks. The company will provide another resource update later this month and has already begun its 2025 drilling campaign to conduct a pre-feasibility study. Shares could fall due to tariffs on U.S. imports, and lower entry points would alleviate many of the concerns about low liquidity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hercules Capital: Buyable, But Proceed With Caution (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Hercules Capital, Inc. (NYSE:HTGC) is one of the most unique players in the business development company ("BDC") sector, as it focuses its investment activities on 4 key sectors: technology, software-as-a-service businesses, life sciences, and sustainable/renewable technologies. My previously published thesis on HTGC was bullish and profitable for a while, but market conditions have shifted since then. Geopolitical tensions, especially on the trade policies, caused the market environment to become much more risky than it used to, especially for the BDC sector.

With that in mind and HTGC's data for Q1 2025 published, I decided to take another look.

Hercules Capital in a nutshell

Hercules Capital is a predominately debt-oriented BDC with 119 portfolio companies in this segment. HTGC focuses on VC-backed businesses and invests from $5m to $500m when it comes to debt investments. It also has a warrant portfolio related to 98 companies, but it only constitutes a ~0.8% of the portfolio.

When it comes to debt investments, first-lien debt represents a dominating (86.6%) part of the portfolio, which I very much like about any BDC during quite turbulent times. That's followed by ~7% share of second-lien debt investments and less material equity investments (3.7%), unsecured debt (1.7%) and other investment vehicles (0.2%). HTGC's debt instruments are (in majority) structured with floating interest rates - 98% of them - leaving the Company susceptible to potential interest changes. For now, HTGC's effective yield regarding debt investments amounts to ~13%.

HTGC proudly highlights compound annual growth rates of total investment income and net investment income amounting to, respectively, 12.1% and 16.1% during the 2015–2024 period, but let's be realistic. Hercules Capital likely will not uphold such a growth dynamic. Take a look at the 2023–2024 period, which was highly influenced by dynamic interest rate increases. I've mentioned earlier that HTGC's debt portfolio is highly susceptible to such changes, so higher interest rates resulted in higher income from debt investments. That's what lead HTGC's NII growth during the last couple of years, and that's not going to repeat (most likely).

Interest rates are more probable to decrease rather than increase further, limiting HTGC's potential to grow NII further. Investors should have that in mind when reviewing such numbers - yes, they are impressive, but unlikely to continue to grow at such a dynamic pace in the years to come.

HTGC's current and potential shareholders have to deal with a double-edged sword the Company wields - valuation. Over the last couple of years, HTGC maintained a significant premium to its net asset value. It recently dropped substantially from ~1.8x to ~1.58x - potentially creating a buying opportunity, but let's not sugar-coat it. A 58% premium is still significant and creates a risk factor for new investors - if anything goes south in terms of HTGC's results or the economic environment, such a large premium will create a material downside risk.

However, high valuation doesn't just provide negatives and risk factors. It could also be considered one of HTGC's most important competitive advantages over less appreciated BDCs. HTGC itself mentions that a substantial premium improves its capital gathering ability, especially when compared to its peer group that tends to trade (on average) below NAV. Here's how it works - the higher the premium, the more proceeds can a given BDC generate through raising equity capital. With higher stock price and higher premium to NAV, HTGC's cost of equity decreases - making it easier to finance new investments and achieve positive returns on equity.

HTGC's value proposition depends on several factors: It can be considered a "buy," but it's better to proceed with caution

Hopefully, the Trump's administration will take reasonable steps in terms of negotiations with key trading partners. If not, there will be an increased risk of an economic downturn, which will inevitably impact HTGC's portfolio companies. Any negative news on that front could put further pressure on HTGC's premium (as we witnessed this year). While the current premium of 58% seems attractive if we judge by 2024–2025 standards, it doesn't look that well when we look at HTGC's metrics throughout, for instance, 2022-2023.

What about the interest rate environment? I often see some confusion about the potential impact of further decreases on BDCs. Yes, recent interest rate cuts put pressure on the interest income debt-oriented BDCs generate, but the story doesn't end there. There are also positives. Firstly, lower interest rates decrease cost of debt BDCs use to finance their activities. Secondly, lower interest rates make businesses more eager to engage in investment initiatives, and therefore, increase their demand for financing. That makes the transactional market more active, providing more deal flow to BDCs - more "supply" enables them to acquire more attractive terms.

Last but not least, think of the environment businesses had to operate in for the last couple of years. High interest rates put a lot of pressure on liquidity and investment initiatives, making it harder for most operations.

Lower interest rates will immediately improve the credit quality of HTGC's portfolio, reducing the risk of non-accruals. And that risk is material - just take a look at the rating difference between 31.12.2024 and 31.03.2025.

Yes, 1-rated businesses increased a bit, look at 2-rated businesses. Their share in the portfolio dropped substantially on behalf of 3-rated ones. While the number of 4-rated companies decreased from 6 to 4, it's safe to assume that the two businesses missing went even lower to 5-rated grading as it increased from 1 to 3 businesses. By the way, the grading means that 1-rated are performing with the highest quality while 5-rated with the poorest. So yeah, HTGC could also benefit from a lower interest rate environment.

That leads me to a conclusion - it seems to me that the premium HTGC trades at is quite high. The economic environment, especially from the perspective of geopolitics, is more risky than it used to be, for instance, a year ago. I see that it could already be somewhat expressed within the premium as it decreased materially, but still - I recommend caution with HTGC. It still holds a lot of potential, and I'm not overly concerned, given its portfolio quality, but for now - I prefer to seize further reinvestments and put HTGC on Hold. Cheers!

data.title: DraftKings: Mixed Bear And Bull Scenarios To Choose From
data.description:
Above: A technical view of the trading pattern.

We looked at a fair value dive into DraftKings' (NASDAQ:DKNG) valuation from several sources, all of which concluded that DKNG was vastly overvalued at $40. Guru Focus, for example, showed an ultra-bearish valuation at $2.72. However, others have price points in the mid to high $50s.

Above: The glory days of 2021 are forgotten by investors now,

Net estimates of total losses '25 to '29: Near $3 billion consolidated. Largely due to a sustained marketing spend on buying new business (buyback yield -2.33%). Revenue gains that add heat to DKNG's annual double-digit revenue growth spurts are attributable to excessive marketing spend. Early mover brand recognition linked to aggressive promos has held DKNG's second-place market share steady over the last five years against FanDuel, from Flutter (FLUT).

Forecasts take into account such elements as averaged interest rate estimates and projected revenue growth (or not) until the terminus year. Calculations include free cash flow estimates for the duration until the terminus year.

For calculation purposes, the WACC discount rate range used: was 6.1% to 8.2%, brought to a 7.15% rate in the formula. Operating margin (-11%). So overall, most DCF or intrinsic value calculations now widely dispersed paint a clear Sell picture to investors, using the valuation sites as key factors in sell decisions. Again, the 12 analysts we checked have price points in the $50s.

But revenue continues to grow, in 5% to 20% annual spurts. The trading range of the DKNG stock is held up by analyst price points, running from a worse case of $28 to the best case of $53.

Why the disparity?

The average price point of 12 analysts is $59.25 over the next 12 months? Clearly a Buy. But using the Peter Lynch fair value formula, you're in sell territory. Let's take a quick look.

Revenue: Well over 33% share of the US market and periodically grabbing the No. 1 spot in a given state in a month with a better hold percentage than FanDuel.

2025: $5b

2024: $4.7b

2023: $3.6b

2022: $2.2b

The simple truth: These values are calculated against recent past setbacks in losses to future value factors just beginning. Analysts making the bull case for DKNG have bought into the accretive value of two acquisitions last year: First, SimpleBet for boosting in-play action, and second, Jackpocket, a prize pool contributed by any number of players who wish to contribute to the kitty. A dime or a dollar, or thousands, is up to you.

SimpleBet is a site that immensely enables in-play betting, the moment-to-moment ability for gamblers to bet on a play before it happens. Jackpocket gives players a prize pool for fun participation. DKNG management says both acquisitions will be highly accretive quickly to the DKNG revenue stream, with a superior margin built in.

CEO Jason Robins predicted last week that his EBITDA for 2025 will reach between $900m and $1 billion for 2025.

Organic growth of the sector through to 2030 - it depends

One is reminded by the oft-told tale of the psychiatrist and the patient: "I lie there talking 45 minutes about all my woes, then asked the doctor, so what do you think, doc?"

And the reply. A few strokes of the chin beard and, "Hmmm... it depends." We have a classic "it depends" reply. Future bullish growth of the sector seems to contradict bearish DCF and intrinsic value loss scenarios.

One answer is my own: Over 30 years of hands-on senior executive experience in the gaming business puts my comfort level with long-term forecasts to fade, beginning with around $50 billion GGR by 2030. So a market share by then of each 10% share would be worth around $5b. So the opening gun goal of ESPNbet at a 20% share target or $10 billion in revenue does not work for us at the moment. I think ESPNbet's growing to 20% share is a bridge too far.

Yahoo estimates sector CAGR at 11.65%, bringing a revenue forecast of $93b by 2030. Less optimistic forecasts show CAGR growth from 2025 to2030 to $33.13b. With the wide gap between DCFs and intrinsic value estimates and dozens of organic growth CAGRs and end 2030 total forecasts, we are loath to make a specific call. Downside lurches are within the risk scenarios: Sudden tax hikes by states (Illinois), a resurgence of marketing spend, even more deep-pocketed entries like Fanatics and, of course, stepped-up spending by late-entry biggies like ESPN.

No matter your own bullish or bearish take on sector growth the ultimate truth prevails: A mixed picture of bullish PTs (the future) vs. bearish sell-type numbers leaves me this impression: If you now own DKNG, stay put another two quarters to validate the bullish outlook price target spread shared by 12 analysts. If the analytics formulae used in valuations breed one too many questionable conclusions, then it's time to sell.

Conclusion

First of all, a decision to buy, hold, or sell a sector leader like DKNG clearly begins not so much with how the metrics hold up against standards of EBITDA and P/E performance. There's solvency under pressure to consider, and performance against peers in the sector. And of course, price relative to peers reflects everything but a steal, you may see what others don't. Or, overvalued but worth a shot anyway, due to a new twist in its technology, a coming product, or a possible transaction.

Also, never forget the heat being stirred up by a stock's hot category, Not quite like AI now, but not that far away either. Remember when DKNG hit the jackpot, rising to $71.98 in March 2021? Investors had the conclusion: The sector is running over the trees to $120 (as one analyst had it) because sports betting is the future of legal gambling today. But, inevitably, the dew left the rose and petals began to waft away.

In the first quarter of this year, there was much selling off of smaller institutional positions. Prior one-year lows were hit by reported losses that brought the stock down to around $28 from the mid-thirties. Right now, it's once more tipping a bit higher into that range. Analysts are looking for it to recover back into the high fifties (PT $59) with best-case scenarios.

There is downside risk built into DKNG's policy of raising the transaction fee by 50 cents, following sector leader FanDuel. The move was made by management in the Illinois market in reaction to state fee hikes. This shows that the platforms challenged by state tax hikes will not go unanswered when levied. The 50-cent fee hike comes off the state's take. It sounds like chump change, which in a sense it is. But this could be the little incident that sparks a longer-term battle over rising gaming taxes, good for no one.

At this point, DKNG is a short-term Hold.

data.title: First Solar: Civilizations Die Before The Sun
data.description:
Since my last First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) analysis, the stock has lost 4.5% in price.

I feel compelled to write this analysis because of potential policy changes in the "Big Beautiful Bill," also known as the OBBBA, which is how I will refer to it here. It's officially titled the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act." I won't be commenting on the possible broad implications of the OBBBA, nor even on its potential impact on the solar industry in general. Instead, I will be commenting on the direct impacts on First Solar, which is arguably America's most important long-term energy company.

The headline data that you need to know is that commercial Investment Tax Credits (ITCs) are proposed to be phased down aggressively, from 60% in 2026 to 20% in 2027 to 0% in 2028. Previously, there was a 30% ITC through 2032, 26% in 2033, 22% in 2034, and 0% from 2035 onward. The possible change is a big headwind for First Solar to deal with, but it wasn't as if investors and management didn't already comprehend that something like this was on the horizon. Residential ITCs are proposed to be eliminated after 180 days of the OBBBA being enacted, but this won't affect First Solar, as the company operates in utility-scale solar power. The OBBBA puts forward a firm-power focus on nuclear, geothermal, and hydro credits extended into the 2030s.

However, positively, transferability and commencement-based eligibility will potentially remain intact, although under tighter conditions. This means developers of solar projects could continue to sell their tax credits to other companies or financial institutions, with tax credit qualification being based on the date of construction commencing, instead of the date of operation. Also, tight Foreign Entities of Concern (FEOC) rules outlined in the OBBBA favor domestic producers. This would be a big win for First Solar, which intends to more than double U.S. capacity from approximately 6.5 GW to 14 GW by the end of 2026. This will raise the company's U.S. share of production from 30% to approximately 55%.

Detailed Financial Implications Reveal Temporary and Surmountable Challenges

Projects scheduled beyond 2027 may become economically unfeasible. This could lead to a near-term acceleration of projects in 2025 and 2026, but a steep drop-off in demand afterward. If the stock becomes richly valued in the medium term, these dynamics could warrant trimming or selling the position, depending on macro demand.

Reduced subsidies also force developers to negotiate aggressively on pricing, which will compress First Solar's profit margins. Many contracts may also be sought for renegotiation now. In light of the new conditions, developers may also end up relying on higher-cost financing, which will place downward pressure on module prices.

However, it's not all negative. Given the heightened competitive environment, weak competitors will exit. This will allow First Solar to gain market share, especially as it already dominates the U.S.-domestic solar production landscape. I reiterate, tight FEOC rules and domestic content requirements in the OBBBA will greatly favor First Solar if enacted. Furthermore, First Solar's low cost per watt, independent supply chain, and superior performance all support the company's project economics even without subsidies.

In essence, management will adapt, and the infrastructure the company has been able to develop with government aid is still absolutely viable to sustain long-term growth through natural industry cycles. The 45X Manufacturing Production Credit is likely to be sustained through 2029 as previously outlined, and phasing down after that; this provides a strong profitability cushion.

A Little Turbulence Never Hurt Anybody

I bought my First Solar position when the stock was trading under the 200-week moving average in price in March 2025, which you can see in the chart below (under the red line). That was exceptional value.

The thing about value investing is that when you master it, you no longer need to quantify anything. You can see all of the valuation parameters in the price chart. Small indicators like the 50-week and 200-week moving averages, as well as the 14-week Relative Strength Index, are useful, but even those are peripheral. There's immense complexity to learning how to do discounted cash flow analysis, discounted earnings analysis, multiple analysis, peer analysis, and many other forms of advanced quantification. Yet, the master looks at the same simple price graph as the novice and sees infinite layers of depth without complex quantification tools. This is a skill that takes countless hours of work to attain and continues to be refined over time. In essence, the art of great investing can be summed up in one word: Wisdom.

However, investing is simple. I will try to save you a lot of time. If the price is under the 50-week moving average in price, you often have a good deal. If it's under the 200-week moving average in price, you often have a deeply undervalued stock. The primary caveat to this is that the fundamental growth trajectory (we can call this the "macro thesis") needs to be intact. In the case of First Solar, it most definitely is. Civilizations die before the sun does. That tells you something: solar power is not going away.

Just like physical suns (and even universes, for that matter), the solar power industry operates in cycles. At the moment, First Solar is benefiting from a current surge in solar demand. In a very conservative base case, Fiscal 2026 normalized earnings per share are going to rise by 50% (likely more). Beyond this, growth will moderate, especially if the tax credits phase out more readily. The point is, that this medium-term cycle isn't over. First Solar stock price is below the 200-week moving average again (deep-value territory). While others sell in fear, I'm inclined to accumulate.

With about $25 in normalized earnings per share for fiscal 2026 and the non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio rising to a conservative 15 (it's currently 12.2 and the five-year average is 20.8), the stock price will be $375. That's my bull-case price target. When operating my portfolio, I'm more defensive than that, and I've set my take-profit to $290. Sentiment can be a fickle thing (it's unpredictable, based on momentum and herd psychology, and very much impacted by news and policy). Even with structural medium-term growth, a substantial valuation re-rating that persists could mean that $250-300 in 12 months' time is more realistic. $290 seems reasonable to me, but still optimistic, which is how I like to operate.

Review Risks for Heightened Awareness, but Be Brave Anyway

The potential phasing down of commercial ITCs means a large amount of utility-scale solar projects could become financially unviable post-2027. As a result of this, projects may accelerate in 2025-2026, with the risk of a sharp demand drop-off beyond 2027 as subsidies disappear.

Under these conditions, developers may push for lower prices, with contract negotiations becoming common. Also, developers may have to access more expensive capital, lowering module demand.

Given such dynamics, First Solar's valuation may overshoot fundamentals in the near term. At $200-250, keep your eye on the ball. If sentiment looks fickle, trim or sell. At $290, it's time to exit, even if it can reach $375 over the next 18 months. It's better to be defensive than sorry. Winners don't gamble, they structure a strategy of investments to succeed under predictable variables.

The current stock price is $145, so if my $290 target is reached, that's a 100% return. There's no need to be greedy here when making money of that magnitude in a cyclical industry. Restraint is the art of the deal.

In the very near term, you need to be aware of price stagnation until the market realizes near-term growth around earnings reports. This is a time to accumulate First Solar stock amid the market's fear and to position for a short-term cyclical upside when the market realizes the 12-month opportunity. Be brave (but not reckless) when weighing this investment in portfolios.

Finally, be agile. If the OBBBA changes and is enacted with more respect for solar's importance to U.S. energy infrastructure in the long term, First Solar's thesis could have more secure protracted potential rather than being a 12-month trade.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

The lesson for investors from this article is not to be reactive to the news or to policy changes. Register them, yes, but realize that policy often can't and doesn't change robust and backlogged demand that much. It may adjust sentiment, and it may revise that growth down, but the growth is still there, and in the case of First Solar, it's still there in spades in the near term. While the market sells out of fear, I accumulate. It's a simple game really, but it's not for the faint of heart. You can't listen to the pack, but need the courage to act when it counts and stick to your convictions under pressure. My $290 is ambitious for 12 months, but with broader 2026 momentum, I posit is on the way with rate cuts and trade tensions easing, it's well within reach.

data.title: Intel: The Silent Threat That No One Is Talking About
data.description:
Despite repeated reassurances in Intel Corporation’s (NASDAQ:INTC) latest earnings update and keynote presentations under new leadership following the appointment of Lip-Bu Tan as CEO, investor conviction in its turnaround prospects remain fragile. The stock has continued to trade resiliently range-bound near the $20-level since Tan’s appointment, while underlying business fundamentals remain prone to Intel Foundry’s inherent capital intensity and the lack of visibility into external adoption.

As a result, we remain incrementally cautious about Intel’s outlook – particularly as its outsized China exposure and concentrated reliance on the Client Computing Group (“CCG”) for growth represent imminent added risk overhangs. This set-up inherently magnifies the company’s vulnerability to tightening U.S. chip export controls. Although existing regulatory restrictions have largely targeted advanced data center chips, the risk of them eventually spilling over into PC processors remains. Any related materialization would directly impact Intel’s CCG segment, where China continues to serve as a critical demand driver.

As discussed in our previous coverage on the stock, Intel also remains increasingly exposed to escalating U.S.-China trade tensions – particularly as the outlook for reciprocal and retaliatory tariffs remains uncertain. While key Chinese OEM partners like Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY) (OTCPK:LNVGF) may eventually mitigate some of the ensuing cost pressures by shifting assembly outside of China, such transitions take time and could weigh on Intel’s near-term sales trajectory.

This would accordingly add to the risk of export restrictions spilling over into PC processors – an outcome that would also directly affect Intel’s reach into the broader China PC market, which remains central to its topline resilience. Our increasing wariness over this potential double whammy to Intel’s already fragile growth outlook is further corroborated by Taiwan’s recent move to add Huawei and SMIC to its own strategic export control list. The development highlights the rising likelihood of coordinated regulatory actions from U.S.-aligned semiconductor hubs – an underappreciated geopolitical risk that Intel’s current fundamental structure remains inherently vulnerable to.

Meanwhile, Intel’s renewed push into AI processors for data center applications under Tan’s leadership – though strategically sound – also introduces incremental investment demands. This could further pressure already-stretched capital requirements tied to ramping Intel Foundry, which continues to lack clarity on long-term returns and partner adoption.

Taken together, we believe Intel remains prone to a potential downward re-rating from current levels, as rising geopolitical and execution risks mount across multiple fronts. Despite the lack of immediate stock reaction, the risks are gradually materializing and have become increasingly difficult to ignore.

Don't Overlook Taiwan’s Newest Export Curbs

As discussed in our recent coverage on Intel, we believe markets continue to underappreciate the extent of the chipmaker’s China exposure – an oversight that introduces meaningful downside risks to the company’s growth outlook. Based on Intel's last revenue by segment disclosure included in its 2024 10K, China accounted for more than 29% of its sales (2024: $15.5 billion). The majority are generated from the sale of client compute processors under CCG. While these products have so far remained outside the scope of tightening U.S. semiconductor export curbs, this structure has yet to meaningfully de-risk Intel from escalating geopolitical headwinds between Washington and Beijing.

This is corroborated by recent commentary from “White House crypto and [AI] czar” David Sacks. Specifically, Sacks’ remarks provide reinforcement that U.S. semiconductor restrictions are primarily driven by national security considerations, with China as the primary target. In reference to the now-shelved “AI Diffusion Framework” proposed under the Biden administration, Sacks noted the rule had imposed overly broad restrictions that risked alienating U.S. allies. In contrast, the Trump administration’s emerging approach is expected to be more focused and deliberate, tightening export curbs with China squarely in mind:

Sacks’s comments highlighted the Trump administration’s intention to maintain pressure on China over technology as the world’s two largest economies vie for an advantage in AI…“The leading American semiconductor should not go to China, but we have export controls on that,” he said. “I don’t think that we need a new globalized regime on every single GPU transaction to achieve the objective.”

Source: Bloomberg News.

This shift highlights the U.S. government’s intent to further limit China’s access to advanced semiconductor technologies, which could expand beyond high-end compute to encompass more commercially significant categories like PC processors. And Intel’s growing emphasis on AI-enabled consumer chips for edge application only adds to this concern. Admittedly, the category’s been largely immune to existing export curbs. But Intel’s growing dependence on the China PC market – where demand has recently rebounded – and the CCG segments’ critical role in shoring up its broader fundamental performance make any adverse policy shifts particularly impactful. Yet, the market appears complacent so far, with Intel shares remaining relatively stable in the $20 range despite worsening geopolitical developments in recent months.

Although the U.S. has yet to formally recommend significant export restrictions on AI PC chips that can materially impact Intel’s near-term outlook, there are growing signs of indirect exposure on this front. Specifically, Taiwan has recently added Huawei and SMIC to its “Strategic High-Tech Commodities” (“SHTC”) Entity List. The latest development effectively bans export of all products, including semiconductor technologies, to these Chinese firms unless a special license approved by regulators is obtained. This is expected to significantly raise the compliance burden, while also introducing new friction in the global semiconductor supply chain.

For Intel, the timing could not be worse. Huawei remains a key customer for Intel in penetrating Chinese PC market opportunities. Specifically, Huawei’s best-selling MateBook series currently features Intel Core Ultra processors. Yet, Taiwan’s latest regulatory update threatens to disrupt this relationship, just as Huawei is gaining ground in the domestic PC market. Fueled by Beijing’s push to phase out the use of “foreign-branded devices” within government agencies, Huawei’s PC shipments in China grew 8% y/y in Q1, extending a multi-quarter expansion streak that brought full year 2024 growth to 15% y/y.

Compounding this exposure is Intel’s current reliance on TSMC (TSM) to fabricate the majority of its PC processors. While upcoming generations like Panther Lake are expected to be manufactured in Intel Foundry’s U.S. fabs on the 18A process node, most of Intel’s current PC processors still flow through TSMC’s facilities. Although TSMC has local facilities in China that could potentially mitigate some of the impact of Taiwan’s recent regulatory changes, the fact that it’s a Taiwan-headquartered company may further complicate its ability to serve Chinese end-markets. This is further burdened by the fact that TSMC’s Fab 10 and Fab 16 located in China are currently based on more mature process nodes. The adverse set-up accordingly restricts Intel’s access to local advanced manufacturing capacity needed to potentially bypass implications from Huawei’s recent addition to the Taiwan SHTC Entity List. As a result, even indirect impacts – including constrained licensing, delays and/or reduced production priority – could introduce material risks to Intel’s supply chain and near-term revenue visibility.

Gauging the Impending Impact on Intel

Taken together, the increasingly adverse regulatory backdrop highlights a growing vulnerability in Intel’s already fragile turnaround. The company’s near-term financial stability continues to hinge primarily on CCG, which is currently Intel’s largest revenue- and income-generating segment. Specifically, CCG currently accounts for 65% of Intel Product revenues and 60% of corporate consolidated revenues as of Q1. The product segment's dominant sales mix at Intel has accordingly yielded leading operating margins of almost 31%, representing the company's key profit generator.

Yet, this exact segment has also become increasingly exposed to intensifying U.S.-China geopolitical tensions. The momentum behind coordinated export controls by the U.S. and its allies, versus Beijing’s domestic tech substitution efforts, result in a double bind on Intel’s growth prospects. Yet despite this convergence of downside risks, the Intel stock has largely remained range-bound, suggesting market has yet to fully price in the chipmaker’s heightened exposure to an increasingly hostile operating environment.

A useful proxy can be drawn from mid-2024, when the U.S. government unexpectedly revoked some of Intel’s exclusive licenses to sell certain products to Huawei. Recall that Intel was awarded an exclusive export license to sell to Huawei under the Trump administration in 2019, which bypasses restrictions on the Entity List. The license effectively allowed Intel to supply more than 90% of Huawei’s CPUs for client PC applications, up from about 50% prior to the arrangement. This exclusivity had effectively allowed Intel to participate in more than half a billion dollars of incremental sales of client CPUs to Huawei at the time, catapulting the chipmaker’s local share against rivals like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). Yet, a portion of this tailwind was severed in mid-2024 when the Biden administration announced the early termination of some of Intel’s exclusive export licenses to Huawei.

While the news initially failed to move the stock meaningfully, we flagged in one of our earlier coverages at the time that investors were overlooking a key risk – Intel’s seemingly protected China exposure, particularly through Huawei, was becoming a liability.

The warning was validated just weeks later. Following Intel’s Q2 2024 earnings update in early August, where the company confirmed a hit to China sales from the license revocation, its shares plunged by more than 30% overnight:

Intel Products revenue was $11.8 billion, up 4% year-over-year. The client business grew 9% year-over-year as the AI PC ramp contributed to higher volume and ASPs, partially offset by export license restrictions communicated during the quarter…Revenue was in line with the range we provided in May after receiving notice of an export license restriction, which negatively impacted our client business in China.

Source: Intel Q2 2024 Earnings Call Transcript.

This sequence illustrates how sudden regulatory changes – especially those tied to China – can materially disrupt Intel’s performance and investor sentiment. It also serves as a cautionary precedent for Intel’s current operating backdrop and regulatory exposure. Given Taiwan’s newly announced restrictions on exports to Huawei, as well as the U.S.’ increasingly aggressive stance on containing China’s pace of advanced tech innovations, the risk of history repeating itself remains high. As a result, Intel remains vulnerable to another share valuation reset should the recent regulatory updates materialize into another downward correction to its near-term fundamental outlook – a risk that markets are likely underappreciating still, given the stock’s stability at current levels suggest a level of complacency.

Fundamental Considerations

Adjusting our prior forecast to reflect Intel’s actual Q1 results and management’s updated forward guidance, we now expect full-year 2025 revenue to decline by approximately -7% y/y to $49.3 billion. This outlook incorporates a modest pickup in Intel Foundry revenue contributions, driven by the internal ramp-up of Panther Lake based on its proprietary 18A node, as well as early-stage external adoption beginning to materialize exiting 2025. However, these incremental tailwinds are expected to be more than offset by intensifying challenges across the Intel Products portfolio, particularly in the Data Center and AI (“DCAI”) segment.

Intel’s been restoring focus on AI R&D for data center applications under Tan’s leadership, which was reiterated during the latest Intel Vision 2025 remarks and implemented in action through the recent slew of new hirings to lead related efforts. But the increasingly competitive landscape remains an immense challenge. It's recently estimated that industry leader Nvidia (NVDA) commands between "70% and 95%" of the data center GPU market, critical for the ongoing AI transformation. This compares to Intel Gaudi 3's extended revenue shortfalls during its ramp-up, which was later corroborated by Intel Products CEO Michelle Johnston Holthaus' acknowledgement and decision to ax the Falcon Shores successor's general availability during her stint as interim co-CEO prior to Tan's appointment:

Many of you heard me temper expectations on Falcon Shores last month. Based on industry feedback, we plan to leverage Falcon Shores as an internal test chip only without bringing it to market. This will support our efforts to develop a system-level solution at rack scale with Jaguar Shores to address the AI data center.

Source: Intel Q4 2024 Earnings Call Transcript.

And even now, with Tan at the helm, Intel has yet to release details on its intended product roadmap curated for AI infrastructure applications. As a result, the durability of related investments in turning that gap around remains to be seen.

Our expectation for impending deceleration facing Intel Products is further reinforced by looming demand risks stemming from ongoing tariff uncertainties – particularly those related to precarious U.S.-China relations, as discussed in the previous coverage. These concerns were later acknowledged and embedded in management’s forward guidance during Intel’s Q1 earnings update:

While we have offsets, including a global, highly diversified manufacturing footprint to help mitigate tariffs, we will certainly see costs increase, and we feel it prudent to anticipate a TAM contraction. The biggest risk we see is the impact of a potential pullback in investment and spending as businesses and consumers react to higher costs and the uncertain economic backdrop.

Source: Intel Q1 2025 Earnings Call Transcript.

In parallel, we expect margin pressures to persist through the remainder of the year for Intel. Admittedly, the company’s recent implementation of cost-cutting measures, including headcount reductions and internal restructuring to enhance operating efficiency, are favorable. But these efforts are likely to be offset by the near-term increase in DCAI R&D, as evidenced by its latest hiring spree in DCAI leadership roles.

Further compounding these pressures are incremental tariff costs expected to begin this summer. Even if the new levies end up being less severe than those initially announced by President Trump in April, the broader semiconductor supply chain remains vulnerable to sector-specific tariffs that’ll inevitably increase operating costs. As Intel management has indicated, such inflationary pressures also risk dampening end-market demand, further constraining Intel’s ability to achieve operating leverage.

The risk of retaliatory tariffs from China also looms large. Given Intel’s extensive China revenue exposure, any escalation in U.S.-China trade tensions could materially affect the company’s near-term cost structure and revenue base. This adds yet another layer of uncertainty to the company’s already fragile fundamental outlook.

Price Considerations

As a result, we are setting our base case price target for Intel at $19 per share (previously $21) to reflect the incremental downside risk stemming from recent regulatory changes.

The price is computed under a discounted cash flow analysis based on Intel’s base case fundamental projections outlined in the previous section. Key assumptions include a 9.3% WACC, reflecting Intel’s capital structure and overall risk profile. To estimate terminal value, we apply a 3.5% perpetual growth rate to projected 2029 EBITDA. This assumption aligns with expected long-term economic growth across Intel’s core markets and reflects the sustainable pace at which its steady-state cash flows – proxied by 2029 EBITDA – can grow into perpetually.

Risks to the Sell Thesis

While we remain incrementally cautious on Intel’s fundamental outlook, there are key risks to our sell thesis that warrant acknowledgement. Admittedly, the following risk considerations do not eliminate the underlying risks tied to Intel’s China exposure, inherent capital intensity at a time of economic uncertainty, and broader execution hurdles. However, they could delay or dampen the anticipated downward re-rating should investor sentiment temporarily shift more favorably.

First, Intel’s intensifying focus on revitalizing its DCAI segment, and driving awareness of its proprietary 18A process node through Intel Foundry under Tan’s leadership could yield upside surprises. If these efforts lead to meaningful traction in securing new external foundry customers and/or winning AI infrastructure design wins, they could materially alter the current growth narrative for Intel. A visible pipeline of commercialized 18A contracts, and/or tangible DCAI revenue inflection from next-generation AI processor deployments represent key opportunities to restoring investor confidence in Intel’s turnaround strategy. This could also unlock incremental valuation re-rating potential, despite prevailing macro and geopolitical risks.

Second, the broader semiconductor sector’s exposure to secular AI tailwinds may drive valuation uplift across the board – including for Intel – regardless of its near-term execution challenges. The market has increasingly priced in long-term TAM expansion from global AI infrastructure buildouts, fueling multiple expansion across the broader technology sector ahead of actual fundamental realization. Intel could simply benefit from this sentiment-driven momentum, especially on the back of headlines tied to new AI opportunities and/or strategic regulatory support for domestic chip manufacturing.

Notably, Intel Foundry currently leads advanced semiconductor manufacturing capacity at scale in the U.S. This remains a potential competitive advantage that could attract renewed investor enthusiasm in the event of escalated geopolitical tensions and/or policy pushes for supply chain resilience. Any such developments could trigger a temporary re-rating, even if the underlying business fundamentals remain weak.

This potential for short-term momentum is even applicable in countering our anticipated implications from Taiwan's recent inclusion of Huawei into its SHTC Entity List. Given Intel Foundry is rapidly ramping up production of advanced nodes at its fabs across the U.S. and Europe, there's potential for it to eventually reallocate some capacity to facilitate Huawei's demands, should its exclusive export licenses permit. Specifically, Huawei's best-selling MateBook laptops are currently incorporating Intel Core Ultra 9 ("Arrow Lake"), which is based on Intel 3. Although much of the product's manufacturing has been outsourced to TSMC, with Intel Foundry only focused on packaging to free up resources for 18A ramp-up, there's potential for Intel to broaden its internal capacity over the longer-term. This could help further mitigate its exposure to growth risks stemming from Taiwan's recent rule change.

Final Thoughts

Given the confluence of structural and macro risks that continue to weigh on Intel’s fundamentals, we remain incrementally cautious about the stock’s near-term performance. The company’s outsized revenue exposure in China leaves it vulnerable to tightening export curbs, and rising tariff uncertainties. Signs of increasingly coordinated regulatory actions from the U.S. and its allies aimed at countering China’s tech advancements, as evidenced in Taiwan’s recent SHTC Entity List updates, also represent an imminent threat to Intel – particularly its CCG segment, which remains central to the company’s near-term financial stability.

Meanwhile, Intel’s capital-intensive efforts to revive Foundry and DCAI still face steep execution hurdles. Although internal restructuring and AI-driven initiatives are underway, visibility into external adoption and ensuing margin improvement at scale remains limited.

Yet despite these mounting pressures, the Intel stock has remained range-bound, suggesting markets may be underestimating the risk of another looming fundamental reset. In the continued absence of clear evidence that Intel is structurally turning a corner, we believe downside risks remain underappreciated, which accordingly leaves the stock vulnerable to a further revaluation lower as macro and execution headwinds continue to unfold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 4 Reasons The Stock Market And The Economy Could Get Ugly Fast
data.description:
With the stock market trading near all-time highs, it seems like many investors are expecting even more gains. The market certainly seems to have upside momentum, and it has been resilient, even in spite of geopolitical risks. However, I think caution is warranted because we have also seen how quickly the market can plunge when the narrative shifts. Almost immediately after the "Liberation Day" tariff announcement in early April, the market sold off significantly for multiple days, creating a roughly 20% rout which panicked many investors.

I believe there is a strong chance of another similar pullback, and maybe one that doesn't rebound as quickly.

I bought stocks in the aftermath of the Liberation Day selloff, as a number of signs indicated levels of extreme fear, and I would likely buy again if another plunge occurs, but I have since sold many stocks into the recent strength. I wanted to take profits and raise cash because the market appears fairly valued now, if not overvalued. I also want to be prepared for another potential downdraft, which could be coming. Here are a few factors that could lead to the next big downdraft, that I am concerned about:

1) The Chart And Valuation Concerns

As the 5-year chart of the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPY) below shows, the market has turned in some very strong gains in recent years. We can clearly see the plunge that occurred after Liberation Day and the subsequent rebound. While the resilience and rapid rebound have been impressive, there are some major concerns that suggest caution is warranted, even in this chart. One of my biggest concerns when it comes to the technicals in this chart is the possibility that the recent rebound back to roughly all-time highs is nothing more than a very bearish "Double Top" formation.

It is too early to say, but if the market fails to break out from current levels in a reasonable time period, a Double Top formation will be confirmed for many investors and that could lead to a selloff. In addition to the concerns I have with the chart, there are also valuation concerns. The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is now trading for just over 24 times earnings. This appears overvalued since the average price-to-earnings multiple over the past few years has been closer to 19 times earnings. This suggests a 20%+ pullback could make sense just in order to get the earnings multiple back to the historical average.

2) Geopolitical Concerns Could Spark A Selloff

The situation with Iran has seemed to escalate, but according to President Trump, there still could be some chance of de-escalation if negotiations to end Iran's nuclear program get back on track. Based on the lack of success with negotiations in the past, I think both sides could just be buying time in order to plan their next move. President Trump is contemplating the use of bunker-busting bombs to take out Iran's largest nuclear site, and Iran is suggesting all-out war if the United States gets involved. Some analysts believe that Iran could retaliate against the United States by activating sleeper cells and calling for terrorist attacks, or potentially cyberattacks.

Escalation could lead to a big spike in the price of oil (CL1:COM), especially if the Strait of Hormuz is blocked or mined. A major and sustained increase in the price of oil could cause stocks to plunge and be a contributing factor towards a stagflationary environment. President Trump has said he will make a decision in the next couple of weeks, which means this geopolitical issue could create volatility or a major drawdown if tensions heat up. Regardless of what President Trump does, Israel seems to be willing to eradicate the potential nuclear threat from Iran on their own and has been extremely effective in achieving their goals so far. Therefore, there is still a chance of escalation, even without U.S. involvement.

3) Tariffs Could Lead To A Downdraft Again

While the market has largely rebounded from the tariff concerns, I believe this remains a major risk for a few reasons. First of all, the impact of tariffs has yet to be truly seen by investors because the tariff increases only occurred after Q1. This means that Q2 and Q3 results could start to show the widespread impact of tariffs when companies announce earnings results as well as guidance.

Another factor to consider is that the U.S. dollar (DXY) has dropped significantly in recent weeks, which could be partially attributed to tariff policies. This drop in the dollar could negatively impact companies that have significant foreign exchange risks. It also appears that tariff policies caused some businesses and consumers to stockpile inventory and goods in order to get ahead of any tariff-induced price increases that many companies are expected to implement this year. That frontloading of demand could be masking weakness in business and consumer spending, which might soon appear when the demand surge abruptly halts in the face of higher prices later this year.

One factor in creating the rebound in the stock market was the delay in some of the highest tariffs, but trade deals seem to be taking longer than expected, and the July 9 deadline is coming up soon. There could be another extension granted, but at some point, deals need to be reached and the lack of deals might result in market volatility since any extensions also extend the uncertainty of trade policies.

4) The Labor Market Is Showing Early Warning Signs

While the unemployment rate remains low, there are signs that the labor market is cooling, and this is often an early warning sign of more weakness to come. Recent data shows that new job openings have hit the lowest levels in many months, and this could mean even weaker data will show up in the coming months.

The job market for recent college graduates already seems to be getting impacted by AI. In addition, many companies in the tech and other industries seem to be taking a page out of the DOGE playbook, which is to do more with less and cut waste. In recent weeks, very healthy tech leaders like Microsoft (MSFT), Autodesk (ADSK), and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), have announced layoffs. Even more companies like Amazon (AMZN) seem to be planning for more layoffs in the coming years as AI and humanoid robots replace humans in many positions.

Another concern is that the Federal Reserve is already too late in cutting rates, or will be too late in cutting rates, as President Trump has suggested. When the Federal Reserve suggests it is data-dependent, that already implies that it could be too late to cut off a potential recession, since by the time the data shows rate cuts are needed, it is often because the economy is already in the early stages of recession.

Historically, the Federal Reserve has also underestimated the increase in unemployment by 2.5%, and this could mean the unemployment rate will get considerably worse from the 4.2% level it holds now. The Federal Reserve is predicting the unemployment rate will hit 4.5% in 2026, but if history repeats and unemployment is 2.5% higher than Fed projections, that means it could hit about 7%. This could cause a major downdraft in the stock market.

The combination of the Fed being too late and underestimating labor market weakness once it gets started is why the stock market often plunges even when the Fed starts cutting rates. As Meeder Investment details below, there is historical precedence for the stock market to plunge by more than 20%, after the first-rate cut:

"Many investors believe the stock market will perform well after the Fed starts cutting interest rates. History tells us this is not necessarily true. Since 1970, more than half of the Fed’s first cuts were followed by declines of more than -20% by the S&P 500 Index. Soft landings are easier said than done. Historically during recessions, the Fed underestimates the increase in unemployment by 2.5%."

Where I Could Be Wrong

While AI seems to already be impacting the job market and leading to layoffs at some companies, it is possible that AI saves the day in terms of the economy by being a net positive for the job market and the economy. It is also possible that the Fed will cut rates soon enough to stave off a potential recession. Fed Governor Waller recently expressed concerns about the jobs market, and he suggested the Fed could cut rates as early as July. The market and the economy have been more resilient than many people expected, and this could continue, which means a recession and market decline could be off the table for now.

In Summary

I believe the market is fairly valued, if not overvalued now. It also appears that the recent rebound in the stock market could be short-lived and nothing more than a technical "Double Top" when considering the chart. It also seems like the market and the economy could get ugly fast when the real impact of tariffs is felt, and when, and if, the labor market cracks grow bigger in the coming months. Geopolitical risks also loom large and could cause a sustained spike in oil prices. There was a rapid decline in stocks after Liberation Day tariffs were announced; this shows the market can turn ugly fast when the narrative and data change. I think this is a time to raise cash and wait for better buying opportunities.

No guarantees or representations are made. Hawkinvest is not a registered investment advisor and does not provide specific investment advice. The information is for informational purposes only. You should always consult a financial advisor.

data.title: RDVY: Outperforms Peers Despite Lower Dividend Yield
data.description:
Overview

Dividend-paying companies can provide a sense of stability through market uncertainty. High-quality dividend-paying companies that have healthy operating margins and free cash flows can continue to provide dividends whether markets are going up, down, or sideways. However, there are thousands of dividend stocks in the world, and it can be difficult to determine whether or not a company has healthy dividend metrics. First Trust Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (NASDAQ:RDVY) makes the process easy and provides a diverse range of exposure to some of the highest quality dividend-paying companies that have the potential for growth.

RDVY has an expense ratio of 0.48% and has an inception dating back to 2014, meaning there's over a decade's worth of operating history to reference. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RDVY has a total return of over 12%. When I previously covered RDVY, I issued a buy rating due to the ETF's resilience throughout different market cycles. The ETF has effectively recovered from the prior market decline in response to President Trump's tariff proposals throughout March and April, which reinforces the idea that RDVY's strategy is quite effective.

Although RDVY focuses on dividend-paying companies, the starting dividend yield is low at 1.6%. Therefore, the appeal of RDVY isn't in the starting income potential. The real magic of RDVY is behind the potential for strong dividend growth that can compound over time.
